clusters.title: ADHD, social confidence and risk of alcohol problems
clusters.summary: Young adults with ADHD who are less confident in social situations may be more likely to use alcohol as a coping mechanism, which puts them at higher risk for problem drinking. This was one of the findings of a study exploring the role of social functioning in drinking behaviors of young adults with ADHD.

items.title: ADHD, social confidence and risk of alcohol problems
items.description: Young adults with ADHD who are less confident in social situations may be more likely to use alcohol as a coping mechanism, which puts them at higher risk for problem drinking. This was one of the findings of a study exploring the role of social functioning in drinking behaviors of young adults with ADHD.

clusters.title: Severe COVID or flu may raise lung cancer risk years later
clusters.summary: A severe case of COVID-19 or influenza could increase the risk of lung cancer later on, according to new research. Scientists discovered that serious viral infections can alter immune cells in the lungs, leaving behind chronic inflammation that may help tumors develop months or years later. The increased risk was seen mainly after severe infections that required hospitalization.

items.title: Severe COVID or flu may raise lung cancer risk years later
items.description: A severe case of COVID-19 or influenza could increase the risk of lung cancer later on, according to new research. Scientists discovered that serious viral infections can alter immune cells in the lungs, leaving behind chronic inflammation that may help tumors develop months or years later. The increased risk was seen mainly after severe infections that required hospitalization. Vaccination, however, appears to prevent the dangerous lung changes.

clusters.title: Microfluidic chip tracks cancer relapse by measuring white blood cell adhesion
clusters.summary: A new microfluidic technology that leverages immune cell behavior is set to transform cancer monitoring, thanks to researchers at UNIST. Led by Professor Joo Hun Kang in the Department of Biomedical Engineering at UNIST, the team has introduced a novel diagnostic chip that analyzes the adhesion properties of leukocytes, or white blood cells, to detect cancer recurrence and evaluate the effectiveness of chemotherapy.

items.title: Microfluidic chip tracks cancer relapse by measuring white blood cell adhesion
items.description: A new microfluidic technology that leverages immune cell behavior is set to transform cancer monitoring, thanks to researchers at UNIST. Led by Professor Joo Hun Kang in the Department of Biomedical Engineering at UNIST, the team has introduced a novel diagnostic chip that analyzes the adhesion properties of leukocytes, or white blood cells, to detect cancer recurrence and evaluate the effectiveness of chemotherapy.

clusters.title: School hours have barely changed since the 1800s. This doesn't suit teenagers' sleep
clusters.summary: This year, students at The King's School in Sydney are starting lessons later on Wednesdays. The start of the usual day has been pushed back from 8:50 a.m. to 9:40 a.m.

items.title: School hours have barely changed since the 1800s. This doesn't suit teenagers' sleep
items.description: This year, students at The King's School in Sydney are starting lessons later on Wednesdays. The start of the usual day has been pushed back from 8:50 a.m. to 9:40 a.m. This is to allow students to do self-directed learning at home or school before formal lessons begin.

clusters.title: Simple blood test can forecast Alzheimer’s years before memory loss
clusters.summary: Scientists have created a blood test that can estimate when Alzheimer’s symptoms are likely to begin. By measuring a protein called p-tau217, the model predicts symptom onset within roughly three to four years. The protein mirrors the silent buildup of amyloid and tau in the brain long before memory loss appears.

items.title: Simple blood test can forecast Alzheimer’s years before memory loss
items.description: Scientists have created a blood test that can estimate when Alzheimer’s symptoms are likely to begin. By measuring a protein called p-tau217, the model predicts symptom onset within roughly three to four years. The protein mirrors the silent buildup of amyloid and tau in the brain long before memory loss appears. This advance could speed up preventive drug trials and eventually guide personalized care.

clusters.title: Why tipping keeps rising and may not improve service
clusters.summary: Why do we tip—even when we know we’ll never see the server again? New research suggests it’s not just about rewarding good service, but about social pressure. Some people tip out of genuine appreciation, while others simply follow the norm.

items.title: Why tipping keeps rising and may not improve service
items.description: Why do we tip—even when we know we’ll never see the server again? New research suggests it’s not just about rewarding good service, but about social pressure. Some people tip out of genuine appreciation, while others simply follow the norm. But here’s the twist: those who truly value great service tend to tip more than average, and everyone else adjusts upward to match them.

clusters.title: A bonobo’s pretend tea party is rewriting what we know about imagination
clusters.summary: A bonobo named Kanzi surprised scientists by successfully playing along in pretend tea party experiments, tracking imaginary juice and grapes as if they were real. He consistently pointed to the correct locations of pretend items, while still choosing real food when given the option. The results suggest that imagination may not be exclusive to humans after all.

items.title: A bonobo’s pretend tea party is rewriting what we know about imagination
items.description: A bonobo named Kanzi surprised scientists by successfully playing along in pretend tea party experiments, tracking imaginary juice and grapes as if they were real. He consistently pointed to the correct locations of pretend items, while still choosing real food when given the option. The results suggest that imagination may not be exclusive to humans after all.

clusters.title: Millions of kids take melatonin but doctors are raising red flags
clusters.summary: Melatonin is now widely used to help children sleep, but scientists say the enthusiasm may be getting ahead of the evidence. A major review found clear benefits for children with conditions like autism and ADHD, yet far less data exists for typical childhood insomnia. Researchers also warn about mislabeled supplement doses and rising accidental ingestions among young kids.

items.title: Millions of kids take melatonin but doctors are raising red flags
items.description: Melatonin is now widely used to help children sleep, but scientists say the enthusiasm may be getting ahead of the evidence. A major review found clear benefits for children with conditions like autism and ADHD, yet far less data exists for typical childhood insomnia. Researchers also warn about mislabeled supplement doses and rising accidental ingestions among young kids. Experts say melatonin should be used carefully and only alongside proven behavioral sleep strategies.

clusters.title: Some babies grasp deception by 10 months, rising fast by 17
clusters.summary: A new study has mapped by age young children's ability to understand and practice deception for the first time—and results indicate many can sense it even before turning 1 year old. The research, led by the University of Bristol and published in Cognitive Development, shows about a quarter of children start to understand deception by 10 months, rising to half at 17 months. By the age of 3, children become more proficient, creative and frequent fabricators, according to the findings.

items.title: Some babies grasp deception by 10 months, rising fast by 17

The research, led by the University of Bristol and published in Cognitive Development, shows about a quarter of children start to understand deception by 10 months, rising to half at 17 months. By the age of 3, children become more proficient, creative and frequent fabricators, according to the findings.

clusters.title: How dark personality traits predict digital abuse in romantic relationships
clusters.summary: A new study reveals how specific malevolent personality traits dictate the exact type of digital abuse a person might commit against a romantic partner, highlighting distinct differences in how men and women use technology to harm others.

items.title: How dark personality traits predict digital abuse in romantic relationships
items.description: A new study reveals how specific malevolent personality traits dictate the exact type of digital abuse a person might commit against a romantic partner, highlighting distinct differences in how men and women use technology to harm others.

clusters.title: Mayo Clinic discovers rare gene mutation that causes fatty liver disease
clusters.summary: Mayo Clinic researchers have identified a rare mutation in the MET gene that can directly cause metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease. The mutation disrupts the liver’s ability to process fat, leading to inflammation, scarring, and potentially cirrhosis. The discovery began with a father and daughter who had the disease without typical risk factors.

items.title: Mayo Clinic discovers rare gene mutation that causes fatty liver disease
items.description: Mayo Clinic researchers have identified a rare mutation in the MET gene that can directly cause metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease. The mutation disrupts the liver’s ability to process fat, leading to inflammation, scarring, and potentially cirrhosis. The discovery began with a father and daughter who had the disease without typical risk factors. Large-scale genomic data suggests similar rare variants may quietly contribute to the disease in many more people.

clusters.title: Women who are open to “sugar arrangements” tend to show deeper psychological vulnerabilities
clusters.summary: A recent study published in the Archives of Sexual Behavior suggests that an openness to sugar relationships relates to deeper psychological vulnerabilities. The findings indicate that early emotional wounds and poor coping skills shape transactional dating preferences.

items.title: Women who are open to “sugar arrangements” tend to show deeper psychological vulnerabilities
items.description: A recent study published in the Archives of Sexual Behavior suggests that an openness to sugar relationships relates to deeper psychological vulnerabilities. The findings indicate that early emotional wounds and poor coping skills shape transactional dating preferences.

clusters.title: Scientists solve the mystery of a vitamin B5 molecule that powers your cells
clusters.summary: Coenzyme A, a molecule derived from vitamin B5, is vital for metabolism throughout the body. Scientists discovered that most of it resides inside mitochondria, yet how it reached these cellular powerhouses was unclear. Yale researchers have now identified the transport system that moves CoA into mitochondria, solving a long-standing biological puzzle.

items.title: Scientists solve the mystery of a vitamin B5 molecule that powers your cells
items.description: Coenzyme A, a molecule derived from vitamin B5, is vital for metabolism throughout the body. Scientists discovered that most of it resides inside mitochondria, yet how it reached these cellular powerhouses was unclear. Yale researchers have now identified the transport system that moves CoA into mitochondria, solving a long-standing biological puzzle. The discovery could help researchers better understand diseases linked to metabolic dysfunction and mitochondrial problems.

clusters.title: A lab mistake at Cambridge reveals a powerful new way to modify drug molecules
clusters.summary: Cambridge scientists have discovered a light-powered chemical reaction that lets researchers modify complex drug molecules at the final stages of development. Unlike traditional methods that rely on toxic chemicals and harsh conditions, the new approach uses an LED lamp to create essential carbon–carbon bonds under mild conditions. This could make drug discovery faster and more environmentally friendly.

items.title: A lab mistake at Cambridge reveals a powerful new way to modify drug molecules
items.description: Cambridge scientists have discovered a light-powered chemical reaction that lets researchers modify complex drug molecules at the final stages of development. Unlike traditional methods that rely on toxic chemicals and harsh conditions, the new approach uses an LED lamp to create essential carbon–carbon bonds under mild conditions. This could make drug discovery faster and more environmentally friendly. The breakthrough was uncovered unexpectedly during a failed laboratory experiment.

clusters.title: This ancient sea creature may already have had a brain
clusters.summary: Advanced 3D reconstructions of the comb jelly’s aboral organ reveal a sensory system far more complex than scientists expected. The organ contains a wide variety of specialized cells and is closely linked to the animal’s nerve network, allowing it to coordinate behavior and orientation in the water. Researchers say it may function as a primitive brain-like center.

items.title: This ancient sea creature may already have had a brain
items.description: Advanced 3D reconstructions of the comb jelly’s aboral organ reveal a sensory system far more complex than scientists expected. The organ contains a wide variety of specialized cells and is closely linked to the animal’s nerve network, allowing it to coordinate behavior and orientation in the water. Researchers say it may function as a primitive brain-like center. The discovery suggests that centralized nervous systems might have evolved independently in different animal lineages.

clusters.title: One in three people carry this brain parasite but the body has a kill switch
clusters.summary: A parasite that may already be hiding in your brain has a shocking survival trick: it can infect the very immune cells sent to destroy it. Yet most people never get sick, and new research from UVA Health reveals why. Scientists discovered that when Toxoplasma gondii invades CD8+ T cells — key defenders of the immune system — those cells can trigger a self-destruct mechanism powered by an enzyme called caspase-8.

items.title: One in three people carry this brain parasite but the body has a kill switch
items.description: A parasite that may already be hiding in your brain has a shocking survival trick: it can infect the very immune cells sent to destroy it. Yet most people never get sick, and new research from UVA Health reveals why. Scientists discovered that when Toxoplasma gondii invades CD8+ T cells — key defenders of the immune system — those cells can trigger a self-destruct mechanism powered by an enzyme called caspase-8. By sacrificing themselves, the infected cells also wipe out the parasite inside them.

clusters.title: Brain scans reveal link between thinner brain cortex regions and higher psychopathic traits
clusters.summary: A team of researchers from Spain was curious to know if people with high psychopathic traits have anomalies in the brain's physical structures, which make them incapable of feeling regret or capable of manipulation and other antisocial behavior. They conducted an experiment in which they interviewed men convicted of intimate partner violence (IPV) and a control group with no history of violence to measure their psychopathic traits, followed by brain scans.

items.title: Brain scans reveal link between thinner brain cortex regions and higher psychopathic traits
items.description: A team of researchers from Spain was curious to know if people with high psychopathic traits have anomalies in the brain's physical structures, which make them incapable of feeling regret or capable of manipulation and other antisocial behavior. They conducted an experiment in which they interviewed men convicted of intimate partner violence (IPV) and a control group with no history of violence to measure their psychopathic traits, followed by brain scans.

clusters.title: Just 5 weeks of brain training may protect against dementia for 20 years
clusters.summary: A simple brain-training program that sharpens how quickly older adults process visual information may have a surprisingly powerful long-term payoff. In a major 20-year study of adults 65 and older, those who completed five to six weeks of adaptive “speed of processing” training — along with a few booster sessions — were significantly less likely to develop dementia, including Alzheimer’s disease, even two decades later. Participants who received the boosted speed training had a 25% lower dementia risk compared to those who received no training, making it the only intervention in the trial to show such a lasting protective effect.

items.title: Just 5 weeks of brain training may protect against dementia for 20 years

In a major 20-year study of adults 65 and older, those who completed five to six weeks of adaptive “speed of processing” training — along with a few booster sessions — were significantly less likely to develop dementia, including Alzheimer’s disease, even two decades later. Participants who received the boosted speed training had a 25% lower dementia risk compared to those who received no training, making it the only intervention in the trial to show such a lasting protective effect.

clusters.title: Childhood trauma leaves a lasting mark on biological systems, study finds
clusters.summary: Psychological stress from childhood adversity doesn't just affect mental health. A new study shows that trauma like family disruption physically damages the immune and metabolic systems of children as early as age 13.

items.title: Childhood trauma leaves a lasting mark on biological systems, study finds
items.description: Psychological stress from childhood adversity doesn't just affect mental health. A new study shows that trauma like family disruption physically damages the immune and metabolic systems of children as early as age 13.

clusters.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation.

items.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
items.description: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation. The finding could help pave the way for earlier diagnosis and more personalized treatments.
